summary: Task 3 ports the Gallery core into :core-runtime with the required hardening patches (HF-token plumbing, Firebase Analytics, Gallery-specific lifecycle/FQN couplings) all correctly removed. Grep confirms zero remaining matches for HF tokens, Authorization/Bearer headers, or Firebase symbols. PendingIntent immutability, the T6 MainActivity FQN guard, and the ProcessLifecycleOwner swap are implemented correctly. However, the ZIP extraction path in DownloadWorker.doWork() is carried over from upstream verbatim and contains a textbook ZipSlip sink (CWE-22): zipEntry.name is concatenated into the destination path with no canonical-path check. Because the worker downloads archives from URLs built out of the allowlist JSON (which Task 4 parses without cryptographic verification) and extracts them into app-external storage, a compromised allowlist entry, a MITM on a weak link, or a replaced HuggingFace revision can overwrite arbitrary files under externalFilesDir (and, via ../../, potentially escape). This is an inherited vulnerability but the port moves it into our tree and must be fixed before Wave 2/3 wiring exposes it to real downloads. Several medium/minor concerns around URL encoding, array-length assumptions, and division-by-zero are noted below.

findings.owasp_category: A01:2021 Broken Access Control / CWE-22 Path Traversal (ZipSlip)
findings.issue: ZipSlip vulnerability. The unzip loop builds the output path as `destDir.absolutePath + File.separator + zipEntry.name` with no canonical-path validation. A ZIP archive containing entries such as `../../../../../../data/data/app.sanctum.machina/files/overwrite.txt` will cause `FileOutputStream(filePath)` to write outside `destDir`. Because `fileName`, `url`, and the ZIP contents originate from the allowlist JSON (parsed by AllowlistLoader in Task 4 with no signature verification) and the network (HTTPS but no pin), a tampered allowlist, a compromised HuggingFace revision, or an attacker on a downgraded link can drop files anywhere the app has write access under externalFilesDir — or escape it entirely via `..`. This is the primary attack surface introduced by the port.

findings.owasp_category: A03:2021 Injection / CWE-20 Improper Input Validation
findings.issue: URL template `https://huggingface.co/$modelId/resolve/$commitHash/$modelFile?download=true` interpolates three fields from the allowlist JSON into a URL with no URL-encoding and no whitelist on allowed characters. If a future (or compromised) allowlist entry carries a `modelId` like `evil.com/a#` or a `modelFile` containing `?token=X&`, the constructed URL can be redirected, have query parameters injected, or be pointed at an arbitrary host (e.g., via `@` authority confusion on some URL parsers). Combined with the ZipSlip finding above, allowlist tampering is the root trust assumption here.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) validate allowlist fields at parse time (Task 4) — modelId must match `^[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+/[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+$`, commitHash must be a 40-char hex SHA, modelFile must not contain `/`, `?`, `#`, `..`, or whitespace — or (b) URL-encode each segment with `java.net.URLEncoder.encode(field, "UTF-8")` before interpolating. Strict allowlist validation is preferable because it prevents the attack rather than making it harder. Add a TODO/test hook now and fold the validator into AllowlistLoader in Task 4.

findings.owasp_category: A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration / CWE-772 Missing Release of Resource
findings.issue: When the ZIP entry is not a directory, `FileOutputStream(filePath)` is opened at a path that assumes the parent directory already exists. If the archive does not contain explicit directory entries (common — `jar`/`zip -r` often omit them) the FileOutputStream throws FileNotFoundException mid-extraction, leaking the ZipInputStream and partially-written files. Less a traditional security issue and more a robustness/DoS one: a crafted archive can force the worker into a retry loop and exhaust storage with the on-disk downloaded .zip that never gets deleted (line 265 is only reached after the loop succeeds).
findings.suggestion: Call `outFile.parentFile?.mkdirs()` before opening the output stream (see fix in the ZipSlip finding). Also wrap the entire unzip block in a try/finally so that `zipIn.close()` and deletion of the source `.zip` happen even on extraction failure; currently a thrown exception leaves the tmp zip on disk forever.

findings.issue: `extraDataFileUrls[index]` and `extraDataFileNames[index]` assume the two comma-separated lists have the same length. They are built independently in DefaultDownloadRepository.downloadModel (lines 102-106) and will stay in sync for today's code paths, but the worker has no defensive check. A future regression where one list becomes empty or contains empty strings throws IndexOutOfBoundsException inside the WorkManager foreground worker — potentially leaving the foreground-service notification dangling. Not directly exploitable externally, but it is an input-validation gap on cross-component data.
findings.suggestion: Add `require(extraDataFileUrls.size == extraDataFileNames.size) { ... }` at the top of doWork, or (better) pass the pairs as a single JSON-encoded list so the contract is structural. Since Data is Bundle-backed and has a size limit, keep the comma-join but validate.

findings.issue: The T6 FQN guard uses `require(fqn.startsWith("app.sanctum.machina."))`. The `require` throws IllegalArgumentException, which propagates out of `buildMainActivityPendingIntent()` → `createForegroundInfo()` → `setForeground()`, failing the whole download. If `:app` ever misconfigures `DefaultDownloadRepository.mainActivityFqn` (typo, refactor), downloads break with no graceful fallback. From a pure security standpoint the prefix check is adequate — null bytes cannot appear in a Java String pulled from a Bundle under Android's Parcel serialization, and any prefix match under `app.sanctum.machina.` is by construction our own package. But the contract should be: invalid FQN → no ContentIntent (already the behavior for ClassNotFoundException), not crash. The current `require` is inconsistent with the ClassNotFoundException branch just below that returns null.
findings.suggestion:
Replace `require(...)` with a defensive check that logs and returns null, matching the ClassNotFoundException branch:

```kotlin
if (!fqn.startsWith("app.sanctum.machina.")) {
  Log.w(TAG, "Refusing to build PendingIntent for unexpected FQN: $fqn")
  return null
}
```

findings.issue: Division by zero: `(downloadedBytes * 100 / totalBytes).toInt()` when `totalBytes == 0L` throws ArithmeticException from inside the progress loop. If a model is misconfigured with sizeInBytes = 0 (the default in `Model`) and no extraDataFiles, the worker crashes every time it tries to update the foreground notification. Upstream-inherited but still a live bug after the port.
findings.suggestion: Guard the division: `val pct = if (totalBytes > 0L) ((downloadedBytes * 100) / totalBytes).toInt() else 0`. Clamp to [0, 100] to avoid overflow if downloadedBytes exceeds totalBytes on a resume.

findings.issue: `HttpURLConnection` with no explicit TLS configuration, no certificate pinning, and default redirect following. On API 24+ cleartext traffic is blocked by the framework's network security config only if `usesCleartextTraffic=false` is set or the default is relied upon. The redirect policy is HTTP-default (`instanceFollowRedirects = true`) which means the server can redirect a `https://huggingface.co/...` download to any other HTTPS host; the app will follow and write whatever bytes are returned. Combined with the ZipSlip finding, this amplifies the blast radius if HF serves a compromised redirect.
findings.suggestion: Phase 1 is a port so treat this as follow-up debt. Phase 2: migrate to OkHttp with a host whitelist on the redirect interceptor (only allow `*.huggingface.co`) and consider a CA pin or subresource integrity check (commit SHA already acts as a weak content binding — verify the SHA-256 of the downloaded file against a value in the allowlist).

findings.issue: `if (modelPath.startsWith("/data/local/tmp")) context.getExternalFilesDir(null)?.absolutePath else null` is a dev-mode escape hatch that loads a model file from `/data/local/tmp` (the world-readable ADB sideload location). This is inherited from upstream Gallery. In a release build a user with ADB access can plant a model there and have the app consume it — not a remote-attacker vector, but a local-trust-boundary surprise. Worth documenting, optionally gating behind BuildConfig.DEBUG.
findings.suggestion: Gate the `/data/local/tmp` branch behind `BuildConfig.DEBUG` or remove it for production. If kept, log a prominent warning so operators/security reviewers notice. Non-blocking for Phase 1.

findings.issue: `workManager.getWorkInfoByIdLiveData(workerId).observeForever { ... }` registers an observer that is never removed. This is a memory-leak / lifecycle concern rather than a classical vulnerability, but in a long-running process it accumulates state per download and can be used (by an attacker that can trigger many downloads) to inflate memory. Inherited from upstream.
findings.suggestion: Switch to a scoped observer or explicitly remove the observer on SUCCEEDED / FAILED / CANCELLED. Non-blocking for Phase 1 but flag for Phase 2 polish.
